hero.tagline: Robots that learn - the modern stack from RL to VLAs
hero.intro: Everything traditional in robotics is hand-engineered: someone wrote the perception code, someone tuned the controller, someone designed the planner. The modern shift is that robots can learn these layers end to end - from reinforcement learning that figures out how to walk in simulation, to imitation learning that copies a human demo, to vision-language-action models like π0 and GR00T that take 'put the dish in the sink' as input and output joint commands. This branch is a tour of that stack with what each technique does, when to reach for it, and code to start with.

sections.heading: When learning beats classical (and when it doesn't)
sections.body: Learned methods aren't a universal upgrade. Classical algorithms still win when you have an accurate model and well-defined task: motion planning for a known arm, A* over a known map, Kalman fusion of well-modeled sensors. Learning wins when the world is too complex to model - contact-rich manipulation, agile locomotion over varied terrain, perception in clutter, and any task where you'd rather collect data than write equations.
bullets: Use classical when: dynamics are known, environment is structured, safety is critical
Use learning when: contact, clutter, dexterity, generalization, or large data exists
Hybrid wins often: classical for the safety-critical loop, learning for the perception or skill on top

sections.heading: Reinforcement Learning - the core idea
sections.body:
RL frames a problem as a Markov Decision Process: at each timestep the agent observes a state s, picks an action a, receives a reward r, and transitions to a new state s'. The goal is a policy π(a|s) that maximizes the expected sum of discounted future rewards. For robotics this is hard because: states are partially observed, actions are continuous, dynamics are unknown, and physical robots are slow to gather data.

The good news: modern algorithms (PPO, SAC, TD3) plus massively parallel simulation (Isaac Lab, MuJoCo MJX) have made it routine to train walking quadrupeds, in-hand manipulation, and drone control entirely in sim, then deploy zero-shot to real robots.
bullets: State, action, reward, transition - the four ingredients of any MDP
Policy π(a|s) - what the agent does. Value V(s), Q(s,a) - how good a state/action is
Reward shaping is the dark art - bad rewards produce 'reward hacking' behaviors
On-policy (PPO) vs off-policy (SAC, TD3) - sample efficiency vs stability tradeoff

sections.heading: Simulators + sim-to-real
sections.body: Real robots take seconds per training step; modern GPU simulators run thousands of environments in parallel at hundreds of thousands of steps per second. The 2026 recipe: train in a GPU sim with heavy domain randomization (mass, friction, motor delays, sensor noise, lighting), then deploy on real hardware. The reality gap closes through randomization plus a small amount of real-robot fine-tuning. NVIDIA's Cosmos and similar video world models are also being used to **synthesize photoreal training data** as a sim-augmentation, not just for world modeling.
bullets: Isaac Lab (built on Isaac Sim 5.1 / 6.0) - GPU parallel sim, photoreal, the production-grade choice for humanoid + arm RL in 2026
MuJoCo + MuJoCo MJX - fast contact physics, JAX-friendly, the de-facto research standard. MJX runs on TPU / GPU
Genesis - GPU sim from the open-source community, ~80M FPS aggregate, growing fast
Brax - JAX-native physics from Google, common in research papers
Gazebo (Harmonic) - physics is good enough, vision is stylized; use it for systems work, not RL training
Domain randomization - vary friction, mass, lighting, textures, camera intrinsics every episode
Synthetic data via Cosmos / Omniverse Replicator - fills perception data gaps that pure-physics sim leaves

sections.heading: PPO + SAC - the workhorse algorithms
sections.body: Two algorithms cover 90% of robot RL. PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) is the on-policy default - stable, easy to tune, scales to massive parallel sims. SAC (Soft Actor-Critic) is the off-policy default - sample-efficient, great for tasks where each rollout is expensive, includes exploration via maximum entropy.

codeBlocks.title: Train PPO on a Gymnasium env (stable-baselines3)
codeBlocks.code:
import gymnasium as gym
from stable_baselines3 import PPO
from stable_baselines3.common.vec_env import SubprocVecEnv

def make_env(): return gym.make('Humanoid-v4')

envs = SubprocVecEnv([make_env for _ in range(16)])
model = PPO('MlpPolicy', envs, n_steps=2048, batch_size=64,
            learning_rate=3e-4, gamma=0.99, gae_lambda=0.95,
            clip_range=0.2, ent_coef=0.0, verbose=1)
model.learn(total_timesteps=10_000_000)
model.save('humanoid_ppo')

codeBlocks.title: SAC for an arm reaching task
codeBlocks.code:
from stable_baselines3 import SAC
from stable_baselines3.common.env_util import make_vec_env

env = make_vec_env('FetchReach-v3', n_envs=1)
model = SAC('MultiInputPolicy', env,
            buffer_size=1_000_000, batch_size=256,
            tau=0.005, gamma=0.95, learning_starts=10_000,
            verbose=1)
model.learn(total_timesteps=1_000_000)

sections.heading: Imitation Learning - learn from demonstrations
sections.body:
Start here: HuggingFace's LeRobot is the de-facto starting point in 2026 for any IL work. One library, one dataset format, one CLI - it ships ACT, Diffusion Policy, π0, VQ-BeT, Pi0-FAST, plus loaders for Open-X-Embodiment, DROID, BridgeData, ALOHA, and the upstream openpi (Physical Intelligence's release of π0 / π0.5 weights). If you're picking up IL from scratch in 2026, do not roll your own training loop - clone lerobot, plug in your demos, train.

The theory underneath: when you have demonstrations, IL is often easier and faster than RL. The simplest form, behavior cloning, treats it as supervised learning: given (observation, action) pairs from an expert, train a network to map observations to actions. The catch is distributional shift - once the robot makes a mistake, it's in a state the expert never visited. DAgger fixes this by interactively querying the expert in those new states.

Modern flavors that ship in production stacks today: Diffusion Policy (denoising diffusion as action sampler - wins on dexterous tasks), ACT (Action Chunking Transformer - what ALOHA uses), Flow Matching policies (used by π0 / π0.5), and conditional BC variants used inside VLAs. The 2024-2025 ALOHA Unleashed result demonstrated that scale matters: 26K demos across 5 bimanual tasks + Diffusion Policy was enough to learn deformable-object and contact-rich manipulation reliably.
bullets: Behavior Cloning - supervised regression on (obs, action) pairs. Limited by distributional shift
DAgger - query expert on robot's actual visited states. Fixes BC drift
Diffusion Policy - diffusion model conditioned on observation, samples action chunks; default for dexterous tasks
ACT (Action Chunking Transformer) - transformer that emits k actions at a time, smooths jitter; what ALOHA / Mobile ALOHA / ALOHA Unleashed run on
Flow Matching - π0 / π0.5's continuous-action approach; smoother and faster than diffusion at inference
ALOHA Unleashed (2024) - 26K demos × 5 tasks proved that scale + Diffusion Policy beats algorithm tinkering
LeRobot (HuggingFace) - one library for ACT, Diffusion Policy, π0, datasets, eval; the modern default starting point
Demos collection: kinesthetic teaching, teleop (VR rig / leader-follower like ALOHA / GELLO), trajectory optimization

codeBlocks.title: Behavior cloning skeleton

sections.heading: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for robots
sections.body: VLMs (CLIP, GPT-4V, Gemini, LLaVA, Florence-2, Qwen-VL) take an image + text and produce text or scores. For robots, VLMs solve the open-vocabulary perception problem: instead of training a YOLO model for every new object, you ask 'is there a red mug on the table?' and get an answer. They're the building block for any system that needs to react to natural-language scene descriptions.
bullets: CLIP - image and text into a shared embedding space; great for similarity / retrieval
Open-vocab detection (Grounding DINO, Florence-2, OWL-ViT) - bounding boxes from text prompts
Open-vocab segmentation (SAM 2, SEEM) - mask anything, optionally text-prompted
Multimodal LLMs (GPT-4V, Gemini, Qwen2-VL) - vision-grounded reasoning

codeBlocks.title: Open-vocab detection with Grounding DINO
codeBlocks.code:
from groundingdino.util.inference import load_model, predict, load_image

model = load_model('GroundingDINO/config/GroundingDINO_SwinT_OGC.py',
                   'weights/groundingdino_swint_ogc.pth')
image_src, image = load_image('table_scene.jpg')
boxes, logits, phrases = predict(
    model=model, image=image,
    caption='red mug. blue book. coffee pot.',
    box_threshold=0.35, text_threshold=0.25)

sections.heading: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
sections.body:
VLAs are the heavyweight bet of modern robotics: one transformer that takes camera images and a language instruction, and emits robot actions directly. The 2026 landscape is crowded and most of the leaders are now open-weight. Physical Intelligence's π0 (Oct 2024, open-sourced Feb 2025) and π0.5 (Apr 2025) introduced flow-matching action heads and open-world generalization. NVIDIA's GR00T N1 (Mar 2025) brought a 2B dual-system architecture for humanoids. Figure released Helix for in-house humanoid stacks. OpenVLA's OFT (Optimized Fine-Tuning) extension delivers 25-50x faster inference and high-frequency bimanual control.

The recipe is the same across all of them: pre-train a VLM (Llama, Gemma, PaliGemma backbones), co-fine-tune on Open-X-Embodiment + your robot's demos, output continuous actions (flow matching) or discrete action tokens (autoregressive). Pick the action head based on whether you need dexterity (flow matching wins) or simple shop-floor pick-and-place (token VLAs are simpler).
bullets: π0 / π0.5 (Physical Intelligence) - flow-matching VLA, fully open-source weights + code, π0.5 generalizes to entirely unseen kitchens / bedrooms
OpenVLA (7B) + OFT - open weights, 25-50x inference speedup over vanilla, fine-tune on your task in hours
NVIDIA GR00T N1 / N1.5 - 2B params, dual-system (System 2 VLM + System 1 motor head), trained on Cosmos-synthesized + real humanoid data
Figure Helix - in-house VLA powering Figure 02 / 03 production lines at BMW
RT-2 / RT-X - the original Google work; superseded in practice by π0-family and OpenVLA
Token VLAs vs flow VLAs: tokens are simple but coarse; flow-matching gives smooth high-frequency continuous actions
Open-X-Embodiment + DROID + LeRobot datasets - the public training mix behind every credible 2026 VLA

codeBlocks.title: Run OpenVLA on a single (image, instruction) pair
codeBlocks.code:
from PIL import Image
from transformers import AutoModelForVision2Seq, AutoProcessor
import torch

processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained('openvla/openvla-7b', trust_remote_code=True)
vla = AutoModelForVision2Seq.from_pretrained(
    'openvla/openvla-7b', torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    low_cpu_mem_usage=True, trust_remote_code=True).to('cuda')

image = Image.open('robot_view.jpg')
prompt = 'In: What action should the robot take to pick up the red mug?\nOut:'
inputs = processor(prompt, image).to('cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
action = vla.predict_action(**inputs, unnorm_key='bridge_orig', do_sample=False)
print(action)  # 7-DoF Δpose + gripper

sections.heading: LLMs as task planners
sections.body: Beyond direct VLAs, LLMs shine as high-level task planners. Code-as-Policies has the LLM emit Python that calls your robot's API. SayCan uses an LLM to score skill candidates against a value function. ReAct loops LLM-in-the-loop reasoning with tool / robot calls. The pattern: you provide the LLM with the robot's available skills, the current scene description, and the task - it returns a plan or a single next action.

codeBlocks.title: Code-as-Policies sketch - LLM emits robot Python
codeBlocks.code:
from anthropic import Anthropic

SKILLS = '''
robot.pick(object_name)        # picks named object
robot.place(location_name)     # places held object
robot.go_to(location_name)     # navigates the base
'''

def plan(task, scene_description):
    client = Anthropic()
    prompt = f'''You control a robot. Available skills:
{SKILLS}
Scene: {scene_description}
Task: {task}
Write Python that completes the task. Output only code.'''
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-opus-4-7',
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}])
    return msg.content[0].text

code = plan('put the red mug in the sink',
            'Counter has a red mug, blue book. Sink is to the left.')
exec(code, {'robot': my_robot})

sections.heading: Diffusion policies - generative behavior cloning
sections.body: Diffusion Policy treats robot actions as samples from a conditional distribution: given the current observation, denoise pure Gaussian noise into a sequence of future actions. This handles multimodality naturally (multiple valid grasps, multiple paths around an obstacle), produces smooth chunked actions, and tops the leaderboards on contact-rich tasks. The cost is inference speed - 5-10 denoising steps per command - but distillation and consistency models are closing that gap.
bullets: Inputs: observation history (images + proprio) - typically last 2 frames
Outputs: action chunk - typically next 16 actions
Backbone: 1D U-Net or transformer, conditioned on observation embedding
DDPM scheduler at training, DDIM at inference for fewer steps
Compute receding-horizon: predict 16, execute 8, predict next 16, etc.

codeBlocks.title: Sketch: train a 1D conditional diffusion policy
codeBlocks.code:
import torch, torch.nn as nn
from diffusers import DDPMScheduler

T = 100  # diffusion steps
sched = DDPMScheduler(num_train_timesteps=T)

class UNet1D(nn.Module):
    # ...standard 1D U-Net conditioned on `cond` via FiLM...
    def forward(self, noisy_actions, t, cond): ...

def train_step(obs_seq, action_seq, model: UNet1D, opt):
    # action_seq: (B, horizon, action_dim)
    cond = encode_obs(obs_seq)                # vision + proprio embedding
    noise = torch.randn_like(action_seq)
    t = torch.randint(0, T, (action_seq.size(0),), device=noise.device)
    noisy = sched.add_noise(action_seq, noise, t)
    pred_noise = model(noisy, t, cond)
    loss = ((pred_noise - noise) ** 2).mean()
    opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
    return loss.item()

@torch.no_grad()
def sample(obs_seq, model, horizon=16, action_dim=7, steps=10):
    cond = encode_obs(obs_seq)
    a = torch.randn(1, horizon, action_dim, device=cond.device)
    sched.set_timesteps(steps)
    for t in sched.timesteps:
        eps = model(a, t.expand(1), cond)
        a = sched.step(eps, t, a).prev_sample
    return a

sections.heading: World models & model-based RL
sections.body:
Model-free RL learns a policy by trial and error. Model-based RL learns a model of the world's dynamics first, then plans inside that learned model - far more sample-efficient. The Dreamer family (V2/V3) trains a recurrent latent world model and a policy entirely in dreams; a quadruped can learn to walk in 1 hour of real experience instead of weeks.

The story changed in 2025-2026 with video-pretrained world foundation models. NVIDIA's Cosmos (7B / 14B) was trained on 9,000T tokens / 20M hours of real-world video and has been downloaded over 2M times by Jan 2026; Cosmos Policy was added to turn those world models into action-conditioned planners. Meta's V-JEPA 2 was pretrained on 1M+ hours of video, then action-conditioned with just 62 hours of unlabeled robot video, hitting 80% zero-shot pick-and-place on real arms across labs. DeepMind's Genie 3 (~11B) generates persistent interactive 3D worlds at 24 fps. Wayve's GAIA-2 (8.4B) is the AV-focused entrant.

The philosophical split: pixel predictors (Cosmos, Genie, GAIA) generate full video frames; representation predictors (V-JEPA) skip pixels and predict in abstract feature space. Over $2B has flowed into world-model startups in Q1 2026 alone (World Labs, AMI Labs).
bullets: Dreamer V3 - latent world model + actor-critic, learns walking robots in 1-2 hours of real experience
TD-MPC2 - model + planning hybrid, the strongest model-based method on most manipulation benchmarks
NVIDIA Cosmos (7B / 14B) - pixel-space video world model trained on 20M hours of real-world video; Cosmos Policy turns it into a planner
Meta V-JEPA 2 - feature-space prediction, 80% zero-shot pick-and-place after only 62h robot fine-tuning
DeepMind Genie 3 (~11B) - real-time interactive 3D world, 24 fps, persistent across long horizons
Wayve GAIA-2 (8.4B) - driving-focused video world model used for AV scenario synthesis
Sample efficiency: 10x to 100x less real data than PPO / SAC for many control tasks
Open question: are pixel-perfect rollouts the right abstraction, or is feature-space prediction enough? Both camps have $1B+ funding in 2026

codeBlocks.title: Train Dreamer V3 on a Gymnasium robot env (open source)
codeBlocks.code:
pip install dreamerv3
# Trains a world model + actor-critic on the latent
python -m dreamerv3.train \
    --configs gym_proprio \
    --task gym_HalfCheetah-v4 \
    --logdir ~/dreamer/cheetah

sections.heading: A practical order to learn
sections.body: If you're starting from scratch, this is the order I'd recommend.
bullets: 1. Linear algebra + probability refresher (3blue1brown, Bishop's PRML chapters 1-2)
2. Deep learning fundamentals - Karpathy's nn-zero-to-hero playlist, FastAI part 1
3. Gymnasium + stable-baselines3 - get PPO running on CartPole, then HalfCheetah
4. Read Sutton & Barto chapters 1-6 alongside the coding
5. Imitation learning - robomimic, ACT, Diffusion Policy on a robot dataset
6. Pick a sim (Isaac Lab or MuJoCo MJX), train one task end-to-end, deploy in sim
7. Read Open-X / OpenVLA / RT-2 papers, fine-tune OpenVLA on your own demos
8. Sim-to-real transfer: domain randomization, system identification, residual learning

sections.heading: Datasets, benchmarks & evaluation
sections.body: Robot ML lives or dies by data. Knowing the standard datasets and benchmarks lets you compare against published results, fine-tune from a sane checkpoint, and avoid retraining from scratch. The 2023-2025 wave of cross-embodiment + teleop datasets means there is now enough public data to train a real VLA from scratch. The center of gravity in 2026 is HuggingFace's LeRobot Hub - it ingests Open-X, DROID, BridgeData, ALOHA datasets in one common format, and most new releases publish there first.
bullets: Open-X-Embodiment - 1M+ episodes across 22 robots from 60 datasets and 34 labs; the foundation for OpenVLA, Octo, RT-X, GR00T
DROID - 76K teleop episodes from 18 institutions; high-quality, diverse scenes
BridgeData V2 - 60K episodes on a WidowX arm; the Berkeley generalization benchmark
RH20T - 110K episodes, 147 tasks across 4 robots, multi-modal sensors
ALOHA / ALOHA Unleashed - bimanual datasets, the staple for diffusion-policy benchmarks
LeRobot Hub - the de-facto 2026 distribution channel; reads Open-X, DROID, ALOHA, BridgeData in one format
RoboCasa - large-scale procedurally-generated kitchen scenes (sim, not real); base for π0.5 training mix
Eval benchmarks: SimplerEnv (sim eval that correlates with real-world success), CALVIN, LIBERO, RLBench, ManiSkill 3

codeBlocks.title: Stream Open-X via tfds - 100s of GB without downloading it all
codeBlocks.code:
import tensorflow_datasets as tfds

# Stream just one constituent dataset from the gs:// bucket
ds = tfds.load('berkeley_autolab_ur5', split='train',
               data_dir='gs://gresearch/robotics')
for episode in ds.take(2):
    for step in episode['steps']:
        img = step['observation']['image']                # (H, W, 3) uint8
        action = step['action']                            # robot-specific
        instruction = step['observation']['natural_language_instruction']
        print(instruction.numpy(), action.shape)

codeBlocks.title: Run SimplerEnv eval against an OpenVLA checkpoint
codeBlocks.code:
pip install simpler-env
python -m simpler_env.evaluation.maniskill2_evaluator \
    --task widowx_carrot_on_plate \
    --policy openvla --checkpoint openvla/openvla-7b \
    --num-episodes 25

sections.heading: Vision foundation models
sections.body: Robotics perception increasingly bootstraps from vision foundation models - large image encoders trained self-supervised on internet-scale data. DINOv2 (Meta) gives general-purpose features that segment, classify, and localize objects with little fine-tuning. Sapiens (Meta, 2024) is human-specific. Florence-2 / CLIP-V2 do open-vocab everything. The pattern: use the FM as a frozen backbone, train a small adapter head for your task.
bullets: DINOv2 - self-supervised ViT, frozen features, drop-in for segmentation / classification
Sapiens (Meta 2024) - human pose, depth, segmentation; SOTA for human-centric tasks
SAM 2 - promptable segmentation (point, box, text); video-aware
Florence-2 (Microsoft) - open-vocab detection, captioning, segmentation in one tiny model
EVA / EVA-02 - strong backbones competitive with DINOv2 at smaller scales
Pattern: freeze FM weights, train small adapter; 100×-1000× less compute than scratch

codeBlocks.title: DINOv2 features for retrieval
codeBlocks.code:
import torch
from torchvision import transforms
from PIL import Image

model = torch.hub.load('facebookresearch/dinov2', 'dinov2_vits14').cuda().eval()
tfm = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize([0.485, 0.456, 0.406], [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
])
x = tfm(Image.open('scene.jpg')).unsqueeze(0).cuda()
with torch.no_grad():
    feat = model(x)        # → (1, 384) embedding
# Cosine-similar features → similar scenes / objects

sections.heading: Real-world RL - beyond sim-to-real
sections.body: Sim-to-real is the 2020-2023 playbook: train in sim with domain randomization, deploy on hardware. The 2024-2026 frontier is real-world RL - train policies directly on the robot. Q-Transformer (DeepMind), Robot Learning at Scale (Google), and ALOHA-2 collect millions of real episodes across many robots. Residual policies start from a sim-trained baseline and use real-world data only to fix the gap.
bullets: Q-Transformer - autoregressive Q-learning over discrete action tokens; scales to 1000+ episodes
ALOHA-2 - bimanual real-world IL; ~100 demos per task is enough for many manipulations
Residual policies - sim-trained base + small real-world correction; sample efficient
Offline RL - RT-X dataset, CQL, IQL; bootstrap from existing logs
World models on real data - Wayve's GAIA-1 trains driving in a learned world model
Practical: start with IL, add RL only when IL plateaus and you can collect 10K+ episodes
